lines.text: Good evening, Madison. It is 19:00. The Purge has commenced.
I'm Quinn Foley. You're listening to PRGE — Purge Radio — the only voice on the air from now until 07:00 tomorrow morning.
We're {{ELAPSED}} into what will be twelve of the longest, strangest hours of your year, and I want you to know right up front: you made the right call staying home.
The broadcasts is live, unfiltered, and we are not going anywhere — even if someone tries to make us.
Let's begin with the weather, because weather is what keeps some of you alive tonight. Caroline, what are we looking at?
Quinn, the clock reads 19:00. We're starting clear, 41 degrees, southwest wind at 8 miles per hour.
That's good news for anyone planning to be mobile tonight — visibility is excellent, 10 miles, no precipitation expected until after midnight.
But I want everyone listening to note this: a cold front is tracking in from the northwest. I'm projecting arrival between 00:30 and 01:30 tomorrow morning.
Temperature drop will be sharp — expect it to plummet from the low 40s down to the 20s within an hour of the front's passage.
Rain transitioning to sleet. Wind gusts potentially up to 25 miles per hour.
What that means for Purge Night: the middle hours — let's say 00:30 to 04:00 — are going to be brutal for anyone caught outside.
I've modeled this against the 2159 cold-front Purge, and activity patterns showed a significant drop once conditions worsened.
Cold is a weapon. Plan accordingly.
Caroline, thank you. So the next four hours are clear sailing, and then Mother Nature steps in. That's useful intel.
Holden, I want to move to the pre-Purge data. What are we working with tonight?
Quinn, good to be here. I've got the full pre-Purge snapshot as of 18:45.
The 2168 baseline is still fresh in everyone's mind — 187 confirmed kills across Madison metro, 412 hospitalizations post-7am, 74 structures damaged, 131 vehicles destroyed.
That's what we're measuring against tonight.

What's different this year: ammunition prices are up another 18% from 2168, which historically correlates with a shift toward bladed and melee weapons — we saw that trend accelerating last year, and I expect it to continue.
Firearms represented 44% of 2168 kills. I'm predicting that number drops to somewhere between 38 and 41% tonight.
What that buys us: slightly lower lethality per incident, since blade work is messier and slower than gunshot wounds.
What it costs us: extended suffering. Longer attack durations. More injuries relative to fatalities.
So we're trading body count for trauma. That's grim math, Holden.
It is. But it's the math. The East Side was the hot zone all of last year — 34 kills, 18% of the total. I'm watching that neighborhood very closely tonight.
Atwood Avenue, Schenk's Corners, the corridor near the airport — those areas have established territorial patterns that tend to persist year over year.
If I had to make a single prediction: East Side goes hot fast, probably within the first two hours.
Tim, you hearing the forecast? We've got clear skies for the opening act, then weather complications. Status on the rig?
All green, Quinn. The antenna is repositioned, the backups are hot, and I've got three geolocation points active in case we need to move the primary transmitter.
Signal strength is optimal across the metro. I'm monitoring for jammer attempts — we logged three signal disruptions last year, all brief, all survivable.
If someone tries to take us off the air tonight, we'll know within seconds, and we will restart. That is the promise.
I appreciate that, Tim. For those just tuning in, PRGE operates on an unlicensed frequency, which means we exist in this narrow legal window only during the Purge itself.
The rest of the year, broadcasting from our location is a federal crime. So the fact that we're here at all is a statement.
A statement to everyone listening: you are not alone tonight. There is a voice on the air. There is someone awake. There is documentation.
Quinn, I'm getting the first atmospheric readings from the Mendota buoy — water temperature is holding at 38 degrees, which is warmer than average for this time of year.
I mention that because Lake Mendota is a factor in Purge nights. People attempt crossing. People drown. At 38 degrees, you have maybe 15 minutes of functional motor control.
When that cold front hits and we drop to 26 degrees air temp, the water is going to be even more lethal.
Caroline, that connects to something I wanted to flag: last year, the Mendota shoreline incidents accounted for 3 confirmed fatalities — all related to that cold front arrival around 00:45.
I'm expecting similar patterns tonight, given the forecast is nearly identical.
People panic. People run. They don't always know the geography. The lake becomes a boundary that kills.
So we've got three hours of clear night, then the temperature drops and the landscape becomes hostile. Anyone out there needs to make their moves early or hole up.
That's the window. Use it or don't, but know what you've got.
One more thing: I'm hearing helicopter traffic overhead already. It's not our surveillance — we're not equipped for that — which means it's either government or private security contractors.
There's been no announcement. No flight plan filed. Nobody's saying whose they are.
That's been the case most years, but I want people to know: if you hear rotors, you're likely being observed, even if not directly targeted.
Mystery helicopters. It's always mystery helicopters on Purge Night.
Are they government overwatch? Corporate asset protection for the wealthy neighborhoods? Nobody ever confirms. They just circle.
Tim, keep monitoring the traffic. If the pattern changes, I want to know immediately.

I've got data on helicopter activity from last year — there were at least four distinct rotorcraft maintaining positions over the Capitol, the West Side residential zone, and the lakefront between 20:00 and 03:00.
Suggests layered surveillance, probably coordinated, probably not public.
The West Side is where 85% of the city's wealth is concentrated. It makes sense they'd be protected from above.
Wind is going to be shifting soon — currently southwest, but as that cold front advances, I'm expecting a rotation to the northwest.
That could affect rotor stability, depending on the aircraft. Might see the helicopters departing or repositioning.
So even the sky is not stable tonight. Good to know.
Let me bring everyone up to speed on what we're going to be doing for the next twelve hours.
PRGE is structured as a news and information broadcast, with hosted segments, live field reporting, music, wellness content, and caller integration.
We have field correspondents deployed across Madison — Tucker Traywick will be providing real-time updates from hot zones as they develop.
We have medical and security advisors standing by. We have emergency routes and shelter information. We have grief counselors.
What we also have is the most comprehensive, unfiltered documentation of Purge Night in this city that exists anywhere.
The government doesn't report this. The news doesn't air. Only PRGE broadcasts the night as it actually happens.
For some of you, that's why you're listening. For others, it's company. For others still, it's proof that somebody is recording the truth.
All of those reasons are valid. Stay with us.
I want to touch on the neighborhood-by-neighborhood breakdown from last year, because geography is destiny on Purge Night.
East Side: 34 kills. That's the baseline we're watching.
Capitol Square and Downtown: 28 kills. The fortification there was breached in 2168 — I'm curious to see if they've upgraded their security perimeter.
Williamson Street — Willy Street — had an armed neighborhood watch mount a successful barricade last year. 22 kills, but they were kept outside the perimeter.

State Street ruins — that's the corridor downtown that's been a dead zone since the 2155 collapse. 19 kills last year, but the worst single event: a coordinated group sweep that killed 14 in 90 minutes.
If I were someone who understood organized violence — which I don't personally, but hypothetically — I'd be paying attention to whether that same group reconvenes tonight.
The neighborhoods that performed best in 2168 — that's North Side with only 9 kills, down 11% from the year before — those areas will likely see similar patterns.
Community organization works. Visible preparedness works. Neighborhoods that demonstrate they're defended tend to see violence flow elsewhere.
It's game theory applied to homicide. The predators go where the prey is vulnerable.
And that's the grim calculus of the Purge — your safety depends partly on your neighbors' visibility as threats.
The more you show you're organized, the more likely violence concentrates somewhere else.
Wealthy neighborhoods have private security, so they're fortified. Organized working-class neighborhoods have community watch. Everyone else barricades and prays.
I'm monitoring all police bands and emergency frequencies, even though official responders aren't technically active until 07:00.
Some neighborhoods keep their own radio traffic going — unofficial dispatch, community coordination — and we can monitor that without interfering.
If a pattern emerges that suggests mass-casualty events or infrastructure failure, I can relay that to the appropriate post-Purge authorities for the morning debrief.
So we're not just broadcasting. We're also documenting for the record.
That's the other side of what PRGE does. We're the institutional memory of what actually happened, not the sanitized version that appears in the official reports.

I want to emphasize something: the statistics we're tracking tonight are real. Every kill count, every incident location, every weather factor — it all matters for understanding how Purge Night actually unfolds.
Most people don't see the night in real time. Most people stay home, locked down, waiting. They don't have this kind of granular data.
By listening to PRGE, you're getting access to information that shapes actual survival calculations. That's not nothing.
The wind is shifting already — I'm seeing a directional change around 19:15, even though the main front won't arrive for several hours.
That's actually typical of this pattern. The front announces itself early through wind shifts and pressure changes.
Current conditions remain clear, but I'd argue anyone planning mobile activity should really move in the next three hours while the weather window is open.
Final word before we move into the first hour proper: if you're listening, you've made a choice tonight.
You chose to stay engaged, to listen, to document, or simply to not be alone.
That choice — choosing connection over isolation, choosing to bear witness — that's the opposite of the Purge's intent.
The Purge says: you are alone, you are unprotected, and the law is suspended. PRGE says: you are not alone. Someone is listening. Someone is recording.
We'll be here the entire twelve hours. We will not shut down. We will not abandon this frequency.
Stay with us. The Purge is here. Purge Radio is on the air.

tickerItems.text: Weather alert: cold front tracking in from northwest, arrival expected 00:30–01:30, temperature drop to 20s, sleet conditions, wind gusts to 25 mph.
Helicopter traffic reported overhead, no official flight plan filed, unconfirmed origin, likely government or private security overwatch.
2168 baseline: 187 confirmed kills across Madison metro. Watch East Side, Capitol Square, State Street corridor as historical hot zones.
PurgeShield panels: verified 18% better structural integrity than 2168 models. Consultation and installation available. 1-800-PURGESHIELD.
